nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef and lagoon environments to approximately 14–30 m depth, particularly among staghorn Acropora and Porites coral formations and coral rubble areas. Two disjunct populations: one in the northern Western Pacific (Ryukyu and Ogasawara Islands of Japan, Philippines) and one in the Rowley Shoals off northwestern Australia (Timor Sea).

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on seaweeds and benthic algae in the wild. In captivity, the One Spot Foxface has been documented readily accepting dried marine algae (nori sheets on a clip), spirulina-based pellets and flakes, and a variety of fresh or blanched vegetables. Keeping a well-fed individual tends to reduce the risk of coral nipping; supplementing with herbivore-formulated foods and seaweed sheets daily is generally advised.

summary: The One Spot Foxface is a reef-associated rabbitfish from the Western Pacific, recognised by its bright yellow body, bold black-and-white head mask, and a large dark blotch on the upper flank. Generally considered peaceful and reef-safe when kept well fed. All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous.
careNotes: The One Spot Foxface (also sold as Blotched Foxface Rabbitfish) is recognisable by its striking coloration: a bright yellow body with the head and front portion white, overlaid by a bold black band running from the mouth to the start of the dorsal fin, plus a large irregular black blotch or eyespot on the centre-to-rear upper flank — the 'one spot' the common name references. Juveniles tend to form large schools in the wild, while adults and subadults are typically observed in permanent pairs, including same-sex pairs; in captivity the species is generally kept singly or as a true pair, as two individuals from the same genus often show aggression toward each other. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous — the venom chemistry has been found to share similarities with stonefish toxin — and can deliver a painful sting. Handle only with a net or aquarium tools, never with bare hands. Reef safety is conditional: a well-fed specimen generally ignores corals, but underfed individuals have been reported to nip at soft corals and LPS (large-polyp stony) corals. Ensuring ample algae is available through nori clips or seaweed sheets, supplemented with herbivore-formulated pellets, is generally advised. Like other rabbitfish, the One Spot Foxface can adopt a mottled, darker camouflage pattern when stressed, sleeping, or newly introduced — this is normal and not a sign of illness. The IUCN currently lists this species as Data Deficient.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not documented for this species. Like other siganids, it is believed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner with planktonic larvae, with spawning cues tied to lunar cycles (new moon aggregations documented in the wild). These requirements make reliable home-aquarium reproduction essentially infeasible. All trade specimens are generally wild-collected.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters: care level moderate, temperament peaceful, reef-safe with caution, herbivore, max size 8 in (~20 cm), min tank 70 US gal (~265 L), temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. Collection page https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/foxface-rabbitfish listed the species; product page https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/one-spot-foxface provided full parameters.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), maximum size (20.0 cm SL), native range (Ryukyu Islands, Philippines, NW Australia Rowley Shoals), wild temperature range 25.4–29.1°C (mean 28.2°C — warmer than the LiveAquaria hobby recommendation; the recorded range is an average of both), depth 1–30 m, reef-associated coral habitat (Acropora and Porites), diet (seaweeds, trophic level 2.7), venomous stout spines, juvenile schooling behaviour transitioning to adult pairs.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Siganus unimaculatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (20 cm TL), two disjunct populations (Ryukyu/Ogasawara/Philippines in the north, Rowley Shoals/Timor Sea in the south), habitat (lagoon and reef to 14 m, Acropora and Porites formations), behaviour (adults and subadults in permanent pairs including same-sex pairs, juveniles in schools), diet (seaweeds, herbivore), reproductive timing (new moon aggregations, demersal eggs), venomous fin spines (chemistry similar to stonefish toxin), IUCN Data Deficient.

fish.breedingNotes: The One Spot Foxface is believed to spawn in open-water aggregations tied to the lunar cycle, with new moon gatherings documented in the wild. Eggs and larvae are planktonic, making reliable captive breeding essentially infeasible in a home aquarium. No captive breeding has been documented, and virtually all trade specimens are wild-collected.
